original_abstract: Established in 1863, the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) has long been regarded as the gold standard of humanitarian intervention. However, despite a legacy spanning more than 150 years, a growing chorus of former officials suggests the organization is currently in decline, fueled by a series of contentious policy shifts. Critics argue that the ICRC has compromised its hallmark neutrality and independence while expanding at a rate that has diluted its operational efficiency, trading its reputation for swift, focused fieldwork for bureaucratic overreach. In this analysis, David P. Forsythe re-examines the ICRC’s strategic decisions over recent decades. He contends that while the organization faces significant challenges and has committed notable errors, talk of its fatal decline is exaggerated. Forsythe suggests that while marginal policy corrections are necessary to restore organizational focus, the ICRC remains a vital global actor whose demise has been predicted prematurely.
humanized_abstract: The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) was created in 1863 and is generally regarded as the most effective and authoritative humanitarian relief organization in the world. A growing number of its former officials have complained in recent years that the institution has been moving in the wrong direction on a whole range of issues, and that the ICRC has lost its neutrality, ceased to be an independent humanitarian voice, and has become a poorly managed institution. It has expanded rapidly into new fields of activity, which has undermined its humanitarian effectiveness and compromised its legend for speed and purposeful action. In this work David P. Forsythe assesses ICRC strategies and actions over recent decades. While acknowledging the ICRC's serious problems and mistakes, he does not agree that the organization is on the brink of disaster as many of its critics have alleged. While modest adjustments in policy would be in order to correct recent aberrations, the ICRC is likely to remain a major humanitarian force for some time to come, and premature predictions of its decline have already proved warranted only in part.
